agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand signals. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving most critical business parameters undefined.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "Undefined measure of delivery request volume or intensity — no definition of how demand is measured, over what time window, or at what geographic granularity" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — parameters entirely undefined" },
      { "term": "Real time", "definition": "Prices adjust as demand changes, not on a batch/scheduled basis — latency tolerance undefined" },
      { "term": "Fully automated", "definition": "No manual human intervention required for price adjustments under normal operation" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business pricing policy. A VP could decide to freeze prices during a PR crisis or promotional period. The direction of adjustment is a strategy choice, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand exceeds the high-demand threshold, when a price is calculated, then the price must be greater than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a competitive strategy policy. Leadership could choose to hold prices steady during low demand instead of discounting. Policy, not physics.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand is below the low-demand threshold, when a price is calculated, then the price must be less than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — the quoted price must reflect current demand conditions, not stale data",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The definition of 'real time' is a latency SLA, not a state integrity constraint. The system doesn't become corrupt if a price is 30 seconds stale — it becomes suboptimal.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand conditions change, when a new delivery price is requested, then the quoted price reflects demand data no older than [undefined SLA]"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual human intervention required in the normal pricing loop",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is an operational policy. A VP could mandate manual approval for price changes above a certain threshold. This is a design preference, not a constraint that protects data integrity.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand conditions change, when prices are recalculated, then no human approval step is required before the new price takes effect"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Guardrails must exist to constrain prices within acceptable bounds — prices must never exceed a defined ceiling or fall below a defined floor",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If prices go negative or astronomically high, the system produces corrupt outputs that violate basic financial integrity. A price of -$5 or $50,000 for a local delivery is a system integrity failure, not a policy disagreement. The EXISTENCE of bounds is an invariant; the SPECIFIC values of those bounds are policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given any demand level, when a price is calculated, then the price must satisfy: floor_price <= calculated_price <= ceiling_price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A calculated delivery price must always be a positive monetary value",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price violates fundamental financial integrity. You cannot charge negative money for a delivery. This is a physical/financial constraint, not a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given any demand conditions and any guardrail configuration, when a price is calculated, then the price must be > 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing function must be monotonically related to demand — higher demand must never produce a lower price than lower demand, all else being equal",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an implicit business expectation from the CEO's description ('when demand is high, prices go up'), but it's a pricing strategy policy. A business could design non-monotonic pricing (e.g., loyalty discounts at certain thresholds). It's a policy, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given two demand levels D1 > D2 with all other factors equal, when prices P1 and P2 are calculated, then P1 >= P2"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The system must balance competitiveness and profitability — the price floor must be at or above the cost of fulfillment to ensure no delivery is priced at a loss",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Pricing below cost of fulfillment means the company loses money on every delivery. While a VP could authorize loss-leader pricing as a deliberate strategy, the system should never ACCIDENTALLY price below cost. The safeguard against accidental below-cost pricing is an integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given the cost of fulfillment for a delivery, when the price floor is configured, then floor_price >= cost_of_fulfillment"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., natural disaster, major event)", "expected_behavior": "Price ceiling guardrail caps the price. Potential ethical/PR concerns with price gouging during emergencies are NOT addressed by the spec." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price floor guardrail prevents pricing below cost or below zero. System must still return a valid price." },
    { "scenario": "Demand data feed becomes unavailable or stale", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Should the system fall back to base price? Freeze at last known price? This is a critical gap." },
    { "scenario": "Rapid demand oscillation (demand flipping high/low within seconds)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Without dampening or hysteresis, prices could thrash, confusing customers who see different prices seconds apart." },
    { "scenario": "Customer is quoted a price, then demand changes before they confirm", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Is the quoted price honored? For how long? This is the 'price lock' or 'quote validity window' question — completely absent from spec." },
    { "scenario": "Geographic granularity conflicts — demand is high in zone A, low in zone B, delivery crosses both", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. No mention of geographic demand segmentation or how cross-zone deliveries are priced." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail ceiling is set below guardrail floor due to misconfiguration", "expected_behavior": "System must reject invalid guardrail configuration. This is a configuration integrity constraint." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price caps exist in certain jurisdictions (e.g., anti-price-gouging laws)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. No mention of legal/regulatory compliance. This is a significant gap for any real-time pricing system." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can increase revenue 10-25% in high-demand periods but risks customer backlash if perceived as gouging. Below-cost pricing due to guardrail misconfiguration could cause significant losses.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand measurement infrastructure, pricing engine with low latency, and guardrail management tooling. Adds operational complexity vs. static pricing.",
    "user": "Customers experience variable pricing which may reduce trust and price predictability. Competitive low-demand pricing could attract price-sensitive customers. Transparency of pricing logic is unaddressed.",
    "risk": "HIGH — spec is critically underspecified. Price gouging optics, regulatory non-compliance, data feed failures, and quote validity gaps all represent material business risks.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "See variable delivery prices. May feel exploited during high demand. May benefit during low demand. Quote validity uncertainty creates friction." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue", "impact": "Revenue optimization potential but requires cost floor accuracy. Incorrect guardrails could cause losses." },
      { "group": "Operations", "impact": "Must provide real-time demand signals. Must manage guardrail configuration. Increased monitoring burden." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Price gouging laws, consumer protection regulations, and price transparency requirements are unaddressed." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will field complaints about price variability. Need clear policies on price disputes and honoring quoted prices." },
      { "group": "Competitors", "impact": "Dynamic pricing may trigger competitive pricing responses. Low-demand discounting may start price wars." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is an informal all-hands soundbite, not a specification. It contains exactly two concrete directional statements ('high demand → higher price' and 'low demand → lower price'), one vague aspiration ('competitive but profitable'), and one hedge ('probably some guardrails'). Nearly every critical parameter — demand measurement, geographic granularity, time windows, guardrail values, quote validity, latency SLA, fallback behavior, regulatory constraints — is completely undefined. This is an idea, not a spec.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices go up when demand is high (explicitly stated)",
      "Prices go down when demand is low (explicitly stated)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though hedged with 'probably')"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What constitutes 'high' or 'low' demand — thresholds undefined",
      "How demand is measured — request volume? geographic density? time-based?",
      "Guardrail parameters — floor, ceiling, rate-of-change limits all undefined",
      "Quote validity / price lock window — completely absent",
      "Geographic granularity of demand and pricing — absent",
      "Fallback behavior when demand data is unavailable — absent",
      "Regulatory and legal compliance requirements — absent",
      "Cost-of-fulfillment relationship to price floor — implied but not stated",
      "Whether 'real time' means seconds, minutes, or sub-second — undefined",
      "Price dampening / hysteresis to prevent thrashing — absent"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Guardrails are mandatory despite 'probably' hedging — treating this as confirmed intent",
      "Price must never go negative or to zero — inferred from financial common sense, not stated",
      "Cost of fulfillment should be the natural price floor — inferred from 'profitable', not stated",
      "Monotonic relationship between demand and price — inferred from directional statements, not explicitly required"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The word 'probably' before guardrails introduces ambiguity about whether guardrails are a hard requirement. For a pricing system, this must be resolved as mandatory — but the CEO's language does not confirm this.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance/Revenue", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["Treat guardrails as mandatory", "Treat guardrails as optional/configurable", "Require CEO clarification"],
      "recommendation": "Treat guardrails as mandatory. No responsible pricing system ships without bounds. Seek explicit confirmation."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No definition of how demand is measured, at what granularity (geographic, temporal), or what thresholds define 'high' vs 'low'. Without this, the core pricing function cannot be specified.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations", "Finance/Revenue", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Define demand as request volume per zone per time window", "Define demand as percentage of courier capacity utilization", "Require product team to specify"],
      "recommendation": "Require product team to define demand measurement explicitly. This is the foundational input to the entire system."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No specification of quote validity / price lock window. If a customer sees a price and demand changes before checkout, what happens? This is critical for customer trust and conversion.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Finance/Revenue"],
      "options": ["Lock price for N minutes after quote", "Always charge current real-time price at confirmation", "Lock price but with expiry and re-quote"],
      "recommendation": "Lock price for a defined validity window (e.g., 5-10 minutes). Charging a different price than quoted is a conversion killer and potential legal issue."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory compliance. Many jurisdictions have anti-price-gouging laws, especially during declared emergencies. A fully automated surge pricing system could violate these laws.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Finance/Revenue"],
      "options": ["Add emergency/disaster demand cap", "Require legal review per jurisdiction", "Add manual override capability for crisis situations"],
      "recommendation": "Require legal review before launch. Add an emergency pricing mode that caps or freezes prices during declared emergencies."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No fallback behavior defined for when demand data feeds fail. A 'fully automated' system with no demand signal cannot price correctly and has no defined degraded mode.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations", "Customers", "Finance/Revenue"],
      "options": ["Fall back to base/static price", "Freeze at last known dynamic price", "Halt order acceptance until data recovers"],
      "recommendation": "Fall back to base price with alerting. Never halt order acceptance — that's a revenue-destroying failure mode."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Competitive but also profitable' is a tension, not a rule. These two goals conflict at the extremes. No guidance on which takes priority when they conflict.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance/Revenue", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Profitability is the hard constraint (never below cost), competitiveness is the optimization target", "Competitiveness is primary (match market rates), profitability is a monitoring concern"],
      "recommendation": "Profitability should be the hard constraint (price floor >= cost). Competitiveness is the optimization goal within profitable bounds."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
